policy_statement: By 2024, begin recording property decarbonization status at time of sale and permit review to ensure compliance with the time-of-replacement policy (BO.2-3).
mechanism_description: The city begins recording each property’s decarbonization status at sale and permit review to support compliance with the time-of-replacement decarbonization policy.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance/compliance system change → ↑ compliance with building decarbonization set → ↓ on-site fossil fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city adds administrative processes to record and disclose a building/property’s decarbonization status during real estate transactions and during permit reviews, creating a compliance checkpoint and support mechanism tied to “time-of-replacement” set.  
2) Mediating physical process: Higher compliance accelerates switching from fossil-fueled end uses (e.g., gas furnaces/water heaters) to electric/low-carbon alternatives and/or efficiency upgrades, reducing combustion of methane/natural gas and associated CO₂ (and some methane leakage upstream).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower direct (Scope 1) building emissions (and potentially lower lifecycle emissions depending on grid carbon intensity), resulting in net GHG reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased implementation of building decarbonization actions at replacement/permit triggers, which reduces fossil fuel use in buildings and thus lowers GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism is primarily an enforcement/compliance-enabling intervention that increases adoption of building decarbonization measures, yielding direct GHG reductions from reduced fossil combustion.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can indirectly support resource efficiency (through improved energy performance) or institutional capacity, the dominant and intended climate outcome is emissions mitigation via decarbonization compliance.  
3) Edge cases applied: If tracking is implemented without any linked decarbonization requirement or enforcement/support that changes equipment choices, climate impact could be negligible; however, the stated mechanism explicitly supports compliance with a time-of-replacement decarbonization policy, keeping mitigation as the dominant category.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that records decarbonization status at sale/permit review specifically to support compliance with decarbonization requirements functions as a mitigation-enabling governance lever whose main effect is increased decarbonization uptake and reduced emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the resulting emissions reductions come mainly from electrification, fuel switching, or efficiency depends on the linked decarbonization policy design and local grid factors and should be determined at the individual policy-evidence level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If used solely for informational/market transparency without enforcement, incentives, or a binding decarbonization trigger, the dominant effect could shift toward “enabling conditions” with uncertain emissions impact; classification here assumes the tracking is operationally tied to compliance with mandated/required decarbonization at replacement/permit review as described.
key_indicators: "begin recording decarbonization status"; "for each property"; "at time of sale"; "permit review"; "ensure compliance"; "time of replacement policy"
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be construed as a regulatory/administrative compliance mandate tied to permitting; classified as information-disclosure because the core action is recording/registries at sale/permit checkpoints rather than specifying a performance requirement or direct mandate in this statement.
